title: Endpoint Hardening Checklist
content:
## Purpose

This procedure provides a comprehensive checklist for hardening Windows and Linux endpoints to reduce the attack surface and increase the difficulty for adversaries to compromise, persist on, and move laterally from endpoints. Hardening is a preventive control that works in conjunction with detection capabilities to provide defense in depth.

## Scope

Applies to all corporate-managed Windows 10/11 workstations, Windows Server 2019/2022, and Linux (Ubuntu, RHEL, CentOS) endpoints. Covers operating system hardening, application control, credential protection, network hardening, and logging configuration.

## Prerequisites

- Group Policy management access (Windows) or configuration management tool (Ansible, Puppet, Chef)
- CIS Benchmark documentation for the target operating system version
- Change management approval for hardening changes
- Test environment to validate changes before production deployment
- Baseline compliance scan results for current state assessment

## Procedure

### Step 1: Operating System Hardening

**Windows Endpoints**:

- [ ] **Patch management**: All endpoints within 30 days of latest cumulative update
- [ ] **Disable SMBv1**: `Set-SmbServerConfiguration -EnableSMB1Protocol $false` or via GPO
- [ ] **Disable LLMNR**: GPO > Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Network > DNS Client > Turn Off Multicast Name Resolution = Enabled
- [ ] **Disable NetBIOS over TCP/IP**: Network adapter settings > TCP/IP > Advanced > WINS > Disable NetBIOS over TCP/IP
- [ ] **Disable WPAD**: GPO or registry `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Internet Settings\Wpad` set to disable
- [ ] **Enable LSA Protection**: `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\RunAsPPL = 1` (prevents unauthorized access to lsass.exe)
- [ ] **Enable Credential Guard**: Requires UEFI Secure Boot, Virtualization Based Security. Protects credential hashes in memory.
- [ ] **Disable WDigest**: `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\SecurityProviders\WDigest\UseLogonCredential = 0` (prevents cleartext password storage in memory)
- [ ] **Set UAC to Always Notify**: GPO > Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options
- [ ] **Disable remote access when not needed**: Disable Remote Desktop, WinRM, and Remote Registry services on standard workstations
- [ ] **Configure Windows Firewall**: Enable on all profiles (Domain, Private, Public). Block inbound by default. Allow only required ports.

**Linux Endpoints**:

- [ ] **Patch management**: All packages within 30 days of latest security updates
- [ ] **Disable unnecessary services**: `systemctl disable <service>` for avahi-daemon, cups, bluetooth, rpcbind, nfs-server (unless required)
- [ ] **Set secure file permissions**:
  - `/etc/passwd`: 644, `/etc/shadow`: 600, `/etc/group`: 644
  - `/etc/ssh/sshd_config`: 600
  - `/boot/grub/grub.cfg`: 600
- [ ] **Configure SSH hardening**:
  - Disable root login: `PermitRootLogin no`
  - Disable password authentication: `PasswordAuthentication no` (require SSH keys)
  - Set `MaxAuthTries 3`
  - Set `ClientAliveInterval 300` and `ClientAliveCountMax 2`
  - Restrict SSH to specific users: `AllowUsers admin1 admin2`
- [ ] **Kernel hardening** (sysctl.conf):
  - `net.ipv4.conf.all.rp_filter = 1` (reverse path filtering)
  - `net.ipv4.icmp_echo_ignore_broadcasts = 1`
  - `kernel.randomize_va_space = 2` (ASLR)
  - `fs.suid_dumpable = 0` (prevent core dumps from SUID programs)
  - `kernel.dmesg_restrict = 1`

### Step 2: Application Control and Whitelisting

- [ ] **Windows AppLocker** or **Windows Defender Application Control (WDAC)**:
  - Block execution from user-writable directories (`%TEMP%`, `%APPDATA%`, `Downloads`)
  - Allow only signed executables from trusted publishers for standard users
  - Audit mode first for 30 days, then enforce
- [ ] **Block script execution for standard users**: AppLocker script rules for `.ps1`, `.vbs`, `.js`, `.wsf`, `.hta`
- [ ] **Constrained Language Mode for PowerShell**: Activated automatically by AppLocker/WDAC Device Guard policies
- [ ] **Linux application control**: Use AppArmor or SELinux in enforcing mode
  - Verify status: `aa-status` (AppArmor) or `getenforce` (SELinux)
  - Set SELinux to enforcing: `setenforce 1` and `SELINUX=enforcing` in `/etc/selinux/config`

### Step 3: Attack Surface Reduction Rules (Windows)

Microsoft Defender Attack Surface Reduction (ASR) rules provide significant protection against common attack vectors.

- [ ] **Block Office from creating child processes**: Prevents macro-based attacks
- [ ] **Block Office from creating executable content**: Prevents dropper behavior
- [ ] **Block JavaScript/VBScript from launching downloaded content**: Prevents script-based downloads
- [ ] **Block execution of potentially obfuscated scripts**: Targets encoded PowerShell
- [ ] **Block credential stealing from LSASS**: Additional protection beyond LSA Protection
- [ ] **Block process creations from PsExec and WMI**: Prevents lateral movement tools
- [ ] **Block untrusted and unsigned processes from USB**: Prevents USB-based attacks
- [ ] **Use advanced protection against ransomware**: Behavioral ransomware detection

Deploy in audit mode for 14 days, review for business impact, then switch to block mode.

### Step 4: Local Administrator Management

- [ ] **Unique local admin passwords**: Deploy LAPS (Local Administrator Password Solution) or Windows LAPS (built-in to Windows 11 23H2+)
- [ ] **Disable the built-in Administrator account**: `net user Administrator /active:no`
- [ ] **Remove standard users from the local Administrators group**: Only IT-managed admin accounts should have local admin rights
- [ ] **Implement tiered administration**: Tier 0 (domain) admin accounts should never log into workstations; Tier 1 (server) admins should not log into workstations; Tier 2 (workstation) admins only for workstations
- [ ] **Configure Admin Approval Mode**: All admin operations require UAC elevation prompt

### Step 5: Credential Protection

- [ ] **Enforce password policy**: Minimum 14 characters, complexity enabled, 90-day maximum age (or use passphrases with no expiration per NIST 800-63B)
- [ ] **Block common passwords**: Use Azure AD Password Protection or custom dictionary filters
- [ ] **Enable MFA**: Require MFA for all remote access (VPN, RDP Gateway, web portals)
- [ ] **Disable NTLM where possible**: Force Kerberos authentication. Monitor NTLM usage first, then restrict.
- [ ] **Protected Users security group**: Add privileged accounts to the Protected Users group (disables NTLM, weak Kerberos encryption, delegation)

### Step 6: Logging and Monitoring Configuration

Hardening without logging creates blind spots.

- [ ] **Enable advanced audit policy** (Windows):
  - Account Logon: Audit Credential Validation (Success, Failure)
  - Logon/Logoff: Audit Logon (Success, Failure), Audit Special Logon (Success)
  - Object Access: Audit File Share (Success, Failure), Audit SAM (Success)
  - Policy Change: Audit Policy Change (Success)
  - Privilege Use: Audit Sensitive Privilege Use (Success, Failure)
  - Process Tracking: Audit Process Creation (Success) with command line logging
- [ ] **Deploy Sysmon** with comprehensive configuration (SwiftOnSecurity or Olaf Hartong baseline)
- [ ] **Enable PowerShell logging**:
  - Script Block Logging: `EnableScriptBlockLogging = 1`
  - Module Logging: Enable with `*` to log all modules
  - Transcription: Enable with a dedicated output path
- [ ] **Linux audit logging**:
  - Install and enable `auditd`
  - Configure rules for: file integrity (/etc/passwd, /etc/shadow), privilege escalation (sudo, su), module loading, network connections
  - Forward logs to SIEM via syslog or agent

### Step 7: Network Hardening

- [ ] **Host-based firewall**: Enable and configure on all endpoints
  - Block inbound connections by default
  - Allow only required inbound services (e.g., RDP only from admin subnet)
  - Block outbound connections to non-standard ports (optional, high impact)
- [ ] **Disable unnecessary network services**: Print Spooler (if not needed), Remote Registry, Windows Remote Management (if not used for management)
- [ ] **DNS-over-HTTPS blocking**: Prevent endpoints from using DoH to bypass DNS monitoring
  - Block connections to known DoH providers at the firewall
  - Windows 11: Configure via GPO to use corporate DNS only

## Compliance Mapping

| Hardening Control | NIST SP 800-53 | CIS Control | PCI DSS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Patch Management | SI-2 | 7.1 | 6.3.3 |
| Application Whitelisting | CM-7 | 2.5 | 6.4.2 |
| Local Admin Management | AC-6 | 5.4 | 7.1 |
| Credential Protection | IA-5 | 5.2 | 8.3 |
| Logging Configuration | AU-2, AU-3 | 8.2 | 10.2 |
| Network Hardening | SC-7 | 4.1 | 1.3 |

## Escalation Criteria

- Hardening control that breaks a business application: Coordinate with application owner for exception documentation and compensating controls
- Unable to deploy LAPS to > 10% of endpoints: Escalate to endpoint management team with risk assessment
- Audit finds endpoints with disabled security controls (EDR, firewall, logging): P2 security incident for investigation

## References

- CIS Benchmarks: https://www.cisecurity.org/cis-benchmarks
- NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: Security and Privacy Controls
- NIST SP 800-63B: Digital Identity Guidelines
- Microsoft Security Baselines: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/security/threat-protection/windows-security-baselines
- DISA STIGs: https://public.cyber.mil/stigs/
